text: The surgeon demonstrates the port setup for a right upper lobectomy, starting with the placement of an eight millimeter camera port at the junction of the sternum and xiphoid. Additional ports include anterior and posterior twelve millimeter ports for stapling, with the posterior port used for the right upper lobe vein and the first branch of the pulmonary artery. The setup includes a fourth arm port near the spine and a twelve millimeter assistant port triangulated between the camera port and medial stapling arm, ensuring adequate spacing for surgical access.

The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm to apply pressure on the superior segment of the right lower lobe while dissecting the posterior hilum. The posterior mediastinal pleura is divided up to the bronchus intermedius, revealing subcranial lymph nodes and the vagus nerve positioned over the esophagus.
The surgeon dissects the subcarinal lymph nodes within the vascular space, with minor bleeding observed during the procedure.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery at the highest setting to dissect the posterior side of the bronchus intermedius and the right upper lobe bronchus. The dissection focuses on the space between these bronchi, targeting the station XI sump lymph node, which is crucial for exposing the hilum from the posterior aspect during a right upper lobectomy.

The surgeon carefully dissects the station XI sump lymph node from the posterior part of the major fissure, taking care to avoid the superior segmental artery and posterior segmental vein of the right upper lobe. The junction between the right upper lobe bronchus and bronchus intermedius is visible, along with the superior segmental vein. The sump lymph node is extracted with gentle spreading and manipulation to avoid excessive bleeding.

The surgeon performs a bloody dissection of the hilar nodes using a zero-degree camera view over the lung. The station 2R and 4R lymph nodes are exposed and dissected as a whole packet, including surrounding fat. Anatomical landmarks visible during the dissection include the superior vena cava medially, the trachea laterally, and the azygos vein inferiorly, with the pericardium marking the deep margin.

The surgeon packs the surgical area with Surgicel and notes the use of Fibrillar in newer procedures. The dissection focuses on the hilum, aiming to expose the first branch of the pulmonary artery, the right upper lobe bronchus, and the right upper lobe vein. The posterior and superior hilum are well dissected, and attention shifts to the medial hilum. The presence of a small ground glass lesion in the middle lobe is noted, emphasizing the need for careful excision with a margin.
The surgeon visually identifies the middle lobe tumor marked with ICG dye and methylene blue, which appear as distinct colors on the tissue surface, aiding in precise localization of the lesion.

The surgeon uses VATS exposure to reflect the lung posteriorly and begins dissecting around the superior pulmonary vein, identifying the junction between the right upper lobe vein and the right middle lobe vein. The interlobar pulmonary artery is visible behind the superior pulmonary vein. The surgeon carefully dissects around these vascular structures using bipolar cautery, ensuring no thermal spread while preserving the middle lobe vein.

The surgeon marks the pulmonary artery with a vessel loop for easy division later. They focus on identifying the pulmonary artery at the confluence of the fissure, which is notably thick. Using bipolar cautery, the surgeon carefully dissects along the pulmonary artery, creating a tunnel between the artery and lung parenchyma. The sponge roll is used to sweep away loose connective tissue, revealing the yellow hue of the pulmonary artery and a small branch vessel.

The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery instrument to dissect through the posterior hilar space, creating a tunnel above the pulmonary artery. A Robnell PVC catheter is placed in the dissected plane, with the pulmonary artery safely below and the posterior fissure above. The robotic stapler with a white load is used to open the fissure, dividing the thin parenchyma.

The surgeon carefully dissects a small branch of the interlobar pulmonary artery extending towards the lower lobe. Metal clips are applied to secure the branch, avoiding larger plastic clips that may not hold effectively.

The surgeon uses hot scissors to open the space around the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery, which is visible entering the posterior segment. Sponge rolls are used for blunt dissection and to manage bleeding. A red rubber catheter is positioned behind the artery, and a gray load stapler is used to divide the artery, revealing the right upper lobe bronchus. The surgeon dissects around the bronchus, noting peribronchial lymph nodes and the truncus anterior, using a tip-up instrument for safe manipulation.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to divide the right upper lobe bronchus, revealing the truncus anterior on the opposite side. There are a few lymph nodes between the bronchus and the truncus anterior, which are exposed from the backside. The truncus anterior appears bifurcated, and the upper lobe vein remains undivided, visible on the other side. The red rubber catheter is positioned safely behind the bronchus, aiding in the isolation of the vessel. The surgical approach allows visualization of the vein behind the truncus anterior, with the vector positioned above the azygos vein for optimal access.

The surgeon uses a gray load stapler to divide the right upper lobe vein, which is marked with a vessel loop and positioned with a red rubber catheter for traction. The truncus anterior has already been divided, allowing for a safe staple fire from the medial stapling port. The pulmonary artery is visible behind the vein, indicating that all vascular structures have been divided. The bronchus is also divided, and attention turns to addressing the fissure, with visible ICG marking the middle lobe tumor and a minor parenchymal injury noted.

The surgeon repairs the incomplete minor fissure, using a blue load stapler to come across the lung parenchyma while staying below the ICG dye marking. A crossing vein is bleeding slightly, and the surgeon uses the Firefly to visualize the dye location. The connective tissue between the pulmonary artery and the lobe is dissected, and a green load stapler is used as the parenchyma thickens. The red rubber catheter defines the safe plane above the pulmonary artery, ensuring protection during stapling. The procedure involves an extended right upper lobectomy, including a portion of the middle lobe, with adenocarcinoma tumors resected.

The surgeon completes an extended right upper lobectomy, including part of the middle lobe, leaving a significant portion of the middle lobe in place. The tumor is placed in an anchor bag for removal. The procedure is described as a fissureless right upper lobectomy, with ICG used to mark the second tumor.
